items.q: What does an increase in entropy of a system correspond to?
items.correct: An increase in the number of ways energy and particles can be arranged — typically a more dispersed/'disordered' state.
items.misconception: Entropy is just a measure of how messy a system looks visually.
items.random_wrong: Entropy measures how much energy a system contains in total.
items.why: The 'disorder' analogy is overused: students take it literally (mess vs tidy) instead of as a count of microstates.

items.q: If a reaction has a negative Gibbs free energy change, what does that mean?
items.correct: The reaction is thermodynamically feasible — it can proceed — but it may still be very slow.
items.misconception: A reaction with negative delta G will always happen quickly.
items.random_wrong: A reaction with negative delta G must be exothermic in every case.
items.why: Students conflate thermodynamic feasibility with kinetics, assuming 'spontaneous' means 'fast' rather than 'energetically allowed'.

items.q: Why can an endothermic reaction still be spontaneous?
items.correct: If the entropy increase (TΔS) is large enough to outweigh the positive ΔH, ΔG can still be negative.
items.misconception: Endothermic reactions are never spontaneous because they require energy input.
items.random_wrong: All endothermic reactions are spontaneous at room temperature.
items.why: Students equate 'exothermic' with 'spontaneous' from GCSE, missing the role of entropy in Gibbs free energy.

items.q: Which species has the same electron configuration as Ar?
items.why: It's Cl⁻. “Na⁺” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Successive ionisation energies of an atom always:
items.why: It's Increase. “Decrease” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
